story:
Notruhk, roused from dozing with his head stuck in the holosphere after a mind-numbing sector-level gnilruc match, shuffles his four feet up the white stone ramp to his son’s nest chamber. Antennae whirling with excitement, the kid begs for a story. They run through the old favorites—Golden Mandibles and the Three Xoak Grubs, The Three Tiny Crustaceans, The Swamp-Fly Who Cried Arachnid—until Notruhk promises a special tale his own father told him, one not on any reader chip.

Once upon a time, far away, humans from a planet called Earth made the first voyage to a neighboring star. In that system, a strange signal pulsed from the fifth planet: a steady, unnatural energy rhythm, too regular to be geological. They landed, followed the beacon, and found a small, purple, cube-shaped object covered in unfamiliar symbols. It looked metallic, felt astonishingly light, and was clearly made by intelligent beings. Thrilled to have proof they weren’t alone, they scoured the planet but found nothing else. Returning home, the crew were celebrated as heroes. Scientists pried, probed, and puzzled. The object’s materials were unknown; its power source, mysterious. Yet it continually absorbed background radiation and released it in faint, measured pulses. After long study, they decided it must be a novel kind of power generator.

They resolved to build their own, scaled up for easier handling. Earth gathered its best minds and finest engineers. For 23 ellipse durations—through shifts of teams and generations of expertise—they labored, substituting their own materials for the alien ones and settling for a crude, giant replica a thousand times larger. By then, Earth was desperate: its aging fusion grids were failing, its energy demands outpacing every known source. Hopes soared—faster ships, limitless power, maybe even a planetary defense, like the Mapzorp outpost cannons that can swat a planetoid. PAAH-BOOM! Notruhk politely vetoes the sound effects, then presses on.

At last, the big day. The planet watched. The President gave a “remember-this-day-forever” speech and, as he pressed the button, added into the microphone, “Across half the galaxy, on a world called Z’va Prime, they see our flare right now.” The massive purple cube glowed, hummed, lifted, and began to spin. Faster and faster, it shot skyward, hovered over the world, then dove back down and—its crude human materials unable to contain the forces the original safely bled away—unleashed energies like a cosmic version of that asteroid smashing Phautogue’s moon. The planet shattered. The end, says Notruhk.

His son wants the how. Notruhk shrugs off the deep physics, comparing it simply to catastrophic impact dynamics the boy already knows. But there’s a twist: this is a true story. Long-range sensors on Z’va Prime had been tracking the toy ever since a careless child lost it on a distant world. The moment Earth’s replica activated, the signal flared across half the galaxy. And the alien artifact the humans found? Not a weapon. Not a generator. A child’s toy—from Z’va Prime, the most advanced world in the galaxy.

“No dung!” the boy blurts.

“Watch the language,” says Dad, amused.

They try for a moral. The boy suggests, “Don’t build a really big toy on your homeworld?” Close enough, but Notruhk lets it go. “Goodnight, son. Don’t let the humans bite.” The child grins, mandibles relaxing as sleep takes him. It’s the best story his dad has ever told—funny, chilling, unforgettable. And that Earth President, promising a day remembered forever? In a way, he was right.
